Made with MyST

5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression

MATH 4210, Chapter 5

Leer en espanol

A football coach wants to know what makes a punter kick far. He measures thirteen college punters on a set of physical tests: right and left leg strength (in pounds, on a leg-press machine), hamstring flexibility, an overall leg-strength score, and hang time. For each punter he also records the average distance of ten punts, in feet. The question is simple to ask and hard to answer with so few kickers: does a stronger leg really buy more distance, or could the pattern he sees be a fluke of thirteen athletes?

Figure 1 plots punt distance against right-leg strength. The trend slopes up, and the fitted line climbs about nine tenths of a foot for every extra pound of strength. That looks like a real effect. But thirteen points is not many. The inference tools from Chapter 3, the ttt test and its confidence interval for the slope, all rest on a chain of assumptions. Either the errors are normal, or the sample is large enough for the normal approximation to save us. With thirteen punters, neither is guaranteed, and there is no way to check normality from thirteen residuals with any confidence.

This chapter answers the question a different way. Instead of assuming a shape for the errors, it uses the computer to shuffle and resample the data thousands of times and watches what happens. Two ideas do the work. A permutation test (Definition 5.1) breaks the link between predictor and response by shuffling, then asks how often pure chance produces a slope as steep as the one you saw. The bootstrap (Definition 5.4) resamples the data with replacement to see how much the slope would bounce around if you could collect the study again. Both replace a fragile assumption with a computation you can always run. By the end you will be able to build each one from scratch, read its output, and say when it agrees with the classical ttt and FFF tests and when it does not.

This lesson at a glance

  • What we are doing: Testing a regression slope and putting a confidence interval on it by shuffling and resampling the small punting dataset on the computer, instead of assuming a shape for the errors.

  • Why we are doing it: With only thirteen punters the normal-theory ttt and FFF machinery of Chapter 3 may not hold, and normality cannot be checked from thirteen residuals.

  • Main objective: Build a permutation test and a bootstrap confidence interval for the slope from scratch, and say when they agree with the classical ttt test and when they do not.

  • What changed from the last chapters: Chapter 3’s inference leaned on the normal-error assumption (2.6 Maximum likelihood under normal errors); this chapter drops it and replaces it with computation, comparing its permutation p-value against Chapter 3’s ttt-test p-value on the same slope.

Learning objectives

By the end of this chapter you will be able to:

  • Explain why normal-theory ttt and FFF inference for a regression slope can fail, and why small samples make the risk worse.

  • State the no-association null hypothesis and describe the exact logic of a permutation test for the slope.

  • Implement the permutation test for the slope from scratch in R and Python, with no special packages, and compute a permutation p-value.

  • Justify the permutation p-value and the plus-one correction, and compare it against the classical ttt-test p-value on the same data.

  • Distinguish case resampling from residual resampling for the bootstrap, and code each one.

  • Construct and interpret percentile and basic bootstrap confidence intervals for the slope.

  • Decide when computational and classical inference agree, and what to report when they disagree.

5.1 When normal-theory inference is on thin ice¶

Intuition¶

Every test from Chapter 3 came with fine print. Its formulas give exact answers only when the errors follow a normal bell curve, or when the sample is large enough to stand in for that. With thirteen punters we have neither, and this section shows that we cannot even check the fine print. Here is that fine print written out in symbols.

Chapter 3 built a confidence interval for the slope as b1±t∗ s{b1}b_1 \pm t^{\ast} \, s\{b_1\}b1​±t∗s{b1​} and tested H0:β1=0H_0: \beta_1 = 0H0​:β1​=0 with the ratio t∗=b1/s{b1}t^{\ast} = b_1 / s\{b_1\}t∗=b1​/s{b1​}, comparing it to a ttt distribution with n−2n - 2n−2 degrees of freedom. That machinery is exact only when the errors εi\varepsilon_iεi​ are normal. When they are not normal, the ttt ratio is still approximately ttt-distributed if the sample is large, because a version of the central limit theorem smooths the estimate toward normality. The catch is the word large. With thirteen punters there is no large-sample rescue, and there is no way to confirm the errors are normal.

You might think you could just check: fit the line, plot the thirteen residuals on a normal quantile plot, and see whether they fall on a straight line. The problem is that thirteen points are too few to tell normal from non-normal. Figure 2 makes this concrete. The top-left panel is the real punting residuals; the other five are genuinely normal samples of the same size, drawn by the computer. Every one of them bends and wiggles away from the straight line. If known-normal data looks this crooked at n=13n = 13n=13, then a crooked plot from real data tells you almost nothing. The normality assumption is simply uncheckable here.

The way out is to stop assuming a distribution and start generating one. If we can build the distribution of the slope under some clearly stated chance mechanism, using only the data in hand, then we can measure how surprising the observed slope is without ever writing down a normal density. That is what the rest of the chapter does.

Try it 5.1

A classmate fits a regression on n=15n = 15n=15 observations, looks at the normal quantile plot of the residuals, sees a slight bend, and declares “the normality assumption is violated, so the ttt-test p-value is worthless.” What is wrong with both halves of that statement?

Solution

First, a slight bend in a quantile plot of only 15 residuals is expected even when the errors are perfectly normal, as Figure 2 shows, so a mild bend is not evidence of a violation. The classmate is reading noise as signal. Second, even if the errors were genuinely non-normal, the ttt-test p-value is not automatically “worthless”; it may still be a decent approximation, and the honest response is to check it against a method that does not need normality, such as a permutation test, rather than to throw it out by decree. The point of this chapter is to replace the argument about normality with a computation.

5.2 The permutation test for the slope¶

Intuition¶

Here is the question a hypothesis test asks, in plain words: if leg strength had nothing to do with punt distance, how often would random chance hand us a slope as steep as 0.902? To answer it we need to see slopes produced by “nothing going on.” We can manufacture them. Keep the thirteen strength values exactly where they are, take the thirteen distances, and shuffle them so that each distance is reassigned to a random punter. Shuffling destroys any real link between strength and distance while keeping both columns of numbers intact. Fit a line to the shuffled data and record its slope. That slope is a sample of “what a slope looks like when there is no association.”

Figure 3 shows one shuffle. On the left, the real data slopes up. On the right, the same distances reattached to random strengths give a nearly flat line. Do the shuffle thousands of times and you get thousands of no-association slopes: their histogram is the null distribution of the slope, built entirely from your own data with no formula and no normality assumption.

Formula¶

Definition 5.1: Permutation test

A permutation test for a regression slope builds the null distribution of the slope by repeatedly reshuffling the response values YiY_iYi​ against the fixed predictor values XiX_iXi​ (a sample without replacement), which breaks any real association, and then compares the observed slope to the reshuffled ones. It tests H0H_0H0​: XXX and YYY are unrelated (β1=0\beta_1 = 0β1​=0) against the alternative that they are related.

The permutation test for the slope works with these pieces. Let the data be pairs (Xi,Yi)(X_i, Y_i)(Xi​,Yi​) for i=1,…,ni = 1, \dots, ni=1,…,n, and let the observed least-squares slope be

b1=∑i=1n(Xi−Xˉ)(Yi−Yˉ)∑i=1n(Xi−Xˉ)2=SxySxx.b_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \bar X)(Y_i - \bar Y)}{\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \bar X)^2} = \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}} .b1​=∑i=1n​(Xi​−Xˉ)2∑i=1n​(Xi​−Xˉ)(Yi​−Yˉ)​=Sxx​Sxy​​.

In words: the slope is the cross-product of XXX and YYY deviations divided by the spread of XXX, exactly as in 2.2 Least squares from first principles. The null hypothesis is

H0:X and Y are unrelated(in particular β1=0),H_0 : X \text{ and } Y \text{ are unrelated} \quad(\text{in particular } \beta_1 = 0),H0​:X and Y are unrelated(in particular β1​=0),

and the alternative is that they are related (β1≠0\beta_1 \neq 0β1​=0). A permutation is a reshuffling π\piπ of the response values that pairs Yπ(i)Y_{\pi(i)}Yπ(i)​ with XiX_iXi​. For each permutation compute the reshuffled slope b1π=∑(Xi−Xˉ)(Yπ(i)−Yˉ)/Sxxb_1^{\pi} = \sum (X_i - \bar X)(Y_{\pi(i)} - \bar Y) / S_{xx}b1π​=∑(Xi​−Xˉ)(Yπ(i)​−Yˉ)/Sxx​. The two-sided permutation p-value is the fraction of permutations whose slope is at least as far from zero as the observed one:

pperm=#{π:∣b1π∣≥∣b1∣}+1(number of permutations)+1.p_{\text{perm}} = \frac{\#\{\pi : |b_1^{\pi}| \ge |b_1|\} + 1}{(\text{number of permutations}) + 1} .pperm​=(number of permutations)+1#{π:∣b1π​∣≥∣b1​∣}+1​.
Definition 5.2: Permutation p-value

The two-sided permutation p-value is the fraction of permutations whose reshuffled slope is at least as far from zero as the observed slope, with a plus one added to both the count and the total for the observed data itself:

pperm=M+1B+1,M=#{π:∣b1π∣≥∣b1∣},p_{\text{perm}} = \frac{M + 1}{B + 1}, \qquad M = \#\{\pi : |b_1^{\pi}| \ge |b_1|\},pperm​=B+1M+1​,M=#{π:∣b1π​∣≥∣b1​∣},

where BBB is the number of permutations drawn. The plus one keeps pperm≥1/(B+1)p_{\text{perm}} \ge 1/(B+1)pperm​≥1/(B+1), so it can never be exactly zero.

In words: the p-value is how often a reshuffle beats or ties the real slope in size, plus one for the real data itself, divided by the number of tries plus one. There are n!n!n! possible permutations, far too many to list (13!13!13! is over six billion), so we draw BBB of them at random and use those. The extra +1 on top and bottom counts the observed arrangement as one of the possibilities, which we explain and justify next.

Derivation (why the permutation test is valid)¶

Theorem 5.3: Validity of the permutation p-value

Under the no-association null H0H_0H0​, in which the response values are attached to the predictor values without regard to them, the observed slope is one draw from the permutation distribution that places probability 1/n!1/n!1/n! on each of the n!n!n! equally likely pairings. The permutation p-value pperm=(M+1)/(B+1)p_{\text{perm}} = (M+1)/(B+1)pperm​=(M+1)/(B+1) is then a valid p-value: it never rejects a true null more often than its stated level, and its smallest attainable value is the floor 1/(B+1)1/(B+1)1/(B+1).

Proof. Suppose H0H_0H0​ holds in the strong sense that the response values were attached to the predictor values without any regard to them. Then the particular pairing we observed is just one of the n!n!n! equally likely ways to match the fixed collection of YYY values to the fixed XXX values. Any statistic computed on a random pairing, here the slope b1πb_1^{\pi}b1π​, therefore has a completely known distribution under H0H_0H0​: it places probability 1/n!1/n!1/n! on each of the n!n!n! arrangements. This is the permutation distribution, and it needs no assumption about the shape of the errors. Normality never appears.

Our observed slope b1b_1b1​ is the value from one particular arrangement, so under H0H_0H0​ it is one draw from that permutation distribution. The exact two-sided p-value is the null probability of a slope at least as extreme in size,

pexact=#{π:∣b1π∣≥∣b1∣}n!,p_{\text{exact}} = \frac{\#\{\pi : |b_1^{\pi}| \ge |b_1|\}}{n!} ,pexact​=n!#{π:∣b1π​∣≥∣b1​∣}​,

which counts arrangements, including the observed one (it satisfies ∣b1π∣≥∣b1∣|b_1^{\pi}| \ge |b_1|∣b1π​∣≥∣b1​∣ with equality). When n!n!n! is too large to enumerate, we estimate pexactp_{\text{exact}}pexact​ by drawing BBB random permutations. Writing MMM for the number of those BBB draws with ∣b1π∣≥∣b1∣|b_1^{\pi}| \ge |b_1|∣b1π​∣≥∣b1​∣, the estimator

pperm=M+1B+1p_{\text{perm}} = \frac{M + 1}{B + 1}pperm​=B+1M+1​

adds the observed arrangement to both the count and the total. This +1 is not a fudge. It keeps the test honest: the observed data is itself a valid arrangement under H0H_0H0​, so it belongs in the reference set, and including it guarantees ppermp_{\text{perm}}pperm​ can never be exactly zero. A test that could report p=0p = 0p=0 would reject H0H_0H0​ with certainty on finite evidence, which is never justified. With the +1, the smallest possible p-value is 1/(B+1)1/(B+1)1/(B+1), and one can show the resulting test never rejects a true null more often than its stated level. ■\blacksquare■

One tidy consequence: because SxxS_{xx}Sxx​ and the XiX_iXi​ do not change when we shuffle YYY, the reshuffled slope b1πb_1^{\pi}b1π​ is just Sxyπ/SxxS_{xy}^{\pi}/S_{xx}Sxyπ​/Sxx​, a fixed positive constant times the reshuffled cross-product SxyπS_{xy}^{\pi}Sxyπ​. So permuting the slope is the same test as permuting the sample correlation, or permuting SxyS_{xy}Sxy​ itself. Whatever monotone version of “association” you prefer, the permutation test gives the same p-value.

R¶

The permutation test is a short loop. Read the data, write a one-line slope function, compute the observed slope, then shuffle and refit BBB times. No package beyond base R is needed.

punting <- read.csv("data/punting.csv")
dim(punting)
fit <- lm(Distance ~ RStr, data = punting)
round(summary(fit)$coefficients, 5)
[1] 13  7
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept) 15.00896   31.35922 0.47861  0.64159
RStr         0.90204    0.21003 4.29474  0.00127

The classical fit gives a slope of 0.902 feet per pound with a ttt-test p-value of 0.00127. Hold on to that number; the permutation test will produce its own p-value to compare against it. Now the from-scratch pieces.

slope <- function(x, y) {
  sum((x - mean(x)) * (y - mean(y))) / sum((x - mean(x))^2)
}
x <- punting$RStr
y <- punting$Distance
b1_obs <- slope(x, y)
b1_obs
[1] 0.9020383
set.seed(4210)
B <- 5000
perm_slopes <- numeric(B)
for (i in 1:B) {
  y_shuffled <- sample(y)              # shuffle distances, keep strengths fixed
  perm_slopes[i] <- slope(x, y_shuffled)
}
count_extreme <- sum(abs(perm_slopes) >= abs(b1_obs))
p_perm <- (count_extreme + 1) / (B + 1)
c(count_extreme = count_extreme, p_permutation = p_perm)
count_extreme p_permutation 
    4.0000000     0.0009998

Out of 5000 reshuffles, only 4 produced a slope as steep as the real one, so the permutation p-value is (4+1)/(5000+1)≈0.001(4+1)/(5000+1) \approx 0.001(4+1)/(5000+1)≈0.001.

Python¶

The same loop in Python with NumPy. rng.permutation does the shuffling; everything else is a handful of array operations.

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

punting = pd.read_csv("data/punting.csv")
print(punting.shape)
fit = smf.ols("Distance ~ RStr", data=punting).fit()
print(fit.summary().tables[1])
(13, 7)
==============================================================================
                 coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Intercept     15.0090     31.359      0.479      0.642     -54.012      84.030
RStr           0.9020      0.210      4.295      0.001       0.440       1.364
==============================================================================
def slope(x, y):
    x = np.asarray(x, float)
    y = np.asarray(y, float)
    return np.sum((x - x.mean()) * (y - y.mean())) / np.sum((x - x.mean()) ** 2)

x = punting["RStr"].to_numpy()
y = punting["Distance"].to_numpy()
b1_obs = slope(x, y)
print(b1_obs)
0.9020382716049381
rng = np.random.default_rng(4210)
B = 5000
perm_slopes = np.empty(B)
for i in range(B):
    y_shuffled = rng.permutation(y)       # shuffle distances, keep strengths fixed
    perm_slopes[i] = slope(x, y_shuffled)
count_extreme = int(np.sum(np.abs(perm_slopes) >= abs(b1_obs)))
p_perm = (count_extreme + 1) / (B + 1)
print("count_extreme =", count_extreme, " p_permutation =", p_perm)
count_extreme = 5  p_permutation = 0.0011997600479904018

R and Python use different random number generators, so they draw different shuffles: R found 4 extreme slopes and Python found 5, giving permutation p-values of about 0.0010 and 0.0012. The two disagree only in the third decimal place, which is Monte Carlo noise, and both point to the same clear conclusion.

Example 5.1: The punting slope by permutation

Question. Is the upward slope of punt distance on right-leg strength stronger than chance would produce, without assuming normal errors?

Intuition. Shuffle the distances against the fixed strengths 5000 times, collect the no-association slopes, and see where the real slope 0.902 falls among them.

Formula. pperm=(M+1)/(B+1)p_{\text{perm}} = (M + 1)/(B + 1)pperm​=(M+1)/(B+1), where MMM counts reshuffles with ∣b1π∣≥∣b1∣|b_1^{\pi}| \ge |b_1|∣b1π​∣≥∣b1​∣.

Computation. The R and Python loops above give M=4M = 4M=4 (R) and M=5M = 5M=5 (Python) out of B=5000B = 5000B=5000, so pperm≈0.001p_{\text{perm}} \approx 0.001pperm​≈0.001. Figure 4 shows the null distribution with the observed slope marked far out in the tail.

Interpretation. A slope as steep as 0.902 almost never happens when strength and distance are unrelated: about one reshuffle in a thousand. So the data give strong evidence that leg strength really does predict punt distance. The permutation p-value 0.001 agrees closely with the classical ttt-test p-value 0.00127, which is reassuring: here the small sample did not break the ttt test, and now we know that without having to trust normality.

Durable skill: Replace an untestable assumption with a computation

The permutation test trades an assumption you cannot check (normal errors from thirteen points) for a mechanism you can state exactly (shuffle the labels) and run on a computer. This move, turning “I hope the assumptions hold” into “I will simulate the null and look,” is one of the most portable ideas in modern statistics. Whenever a formula-based test rests on a premise you cannot verify, ask whether you can instead generate the null distribution directly by resampling or shuffling. To practice, take any two-group comparison you meet and build its permutation test in ten lines before reaching for a named procedure.

Try it 5.2

In the permutation loop, why do we shuffle only the response y and leave x untouched? Would shuffling x instead, or shuffling both, change the test?

Solution

The null hypothesis is that the pairing between XXX and YYY carries no information, so we destroy the pairing by randomly rematching the two columns. It does not matter which column you shuffle: shuffling YYY against fixed XXX, shuffling XXX against fixed YYY, or shuffling both, all produce the same set of random pairings and the same permutation distribution of the slope, because the slope depends only on how the XXX and YYY values are matched up. We shuffle YYY by convention. What you must not do is shuffle the two columns together (a paired shuffle), because that keeps every original pair intact and tests nothing.

5.3 Permutation on Toluca, and how it compares with the t test¶

The punting example ended in a near-tie between the permutation and ttt-test p-values. To see the two methods on a case where the signal is overwhelming, return to the Toluca Company data from Chapters 2 and 3: the labor hours to produce a part against the lot size, with the least-squares slope b1=3.5702b_1 = 3.5702b1​=3.5702 hours per unit (2.2 Least squares from first principles). Chapter 3 tested that slope with a ttt test and found a p-value of about 4.45×10−104.45 \times 10^{-10}4.45×10−10 (3.7 The F test and its equivalence to the t test, where the ttt and FFF tests coincide for a single slope). We now run the permutation test on the same slope and compare.

toluca <- read.csv("data/toluca.csv")
xt <- toluca$lotsize
yt <- toluca$hours
b1_toluca <- slope(xt, yt)
p_t <- summary(lm(hours ~ lotsize, data = toluca))$coefficients["lotsize", "Pr(>|t|)"]

set.seed(4210)
perm_toluca <- numeric(B)
for (i in 1:B) perm_toluca[i] <- slope(xt, sample(yt))
c(b1_toluca      = b1_toluca,
  perm_count     = sum(abs(perm_toluca) >= abs(b1_toluca)),
  max_perm_slope = max(abs(perm_toluca)),
  t_test_p_value = p_t)
     b1_toluca     perm_count max_perm_slope t_test_p_value 
  3.570202e+00   0.000000e+00   2.767172e+00   4.448828e-10 
toluca = pd.read_csv("data/toluca.csv")
xt = toluca["lotsize"].to_numpy()
yt = toluca["hours"].to_numpy()
b1_toluca = slope(xt, yt)
p_t = smf.ols("hours ~ lotsize", data=toluca).fit().pvalues["lotsize"]

rng = np.random.default_rng(4210)
perm_toluca = np.array([slope(xt, rng.permutation(yt)) for _ in range(B)])
print("b1_toluca      =", b1_toluca)
print("perm_count     =", int(np.sum(np.abs(perm_toluca) >= abs(b1_toluca))))
print("max_perm_slope =", np.max(np.abs(perm_toluca)))
print("t_test_p_value =", p_t)
b1_toluca      = 3.57020202020202
perm_count     = 0
max_perm_slope = 2.7454545454545456
t_test_p_value = 4.4488275871889375e-10
Example 5.2: The Toluca slope by permutation

Question. How does the permutation test judge the Toluca slope, and can it match the ttt test’s tiny p-value?

Intuition. Shuffle the hours against the fixed lot sizes and see whether any reshuffle comes near the real slope 3.5702.

Formula. pperm=(M+1)/(B+1)p_{\text{perm}} = (M + 1)/(B + 1)pperm​=(M+1)/(B+1), as before.

Computation. In 5000 reshuffles the largest slope that chance produced was about 2.77 in size (R) or 2.75 (Python), nowhere near the observed 3.5702. So M=0M = 0M=0 and

pperm=0+15000+1=15001≈0.0002.p_{\text{perm}} = \frac{0 + 1}{5000 + 1} = \frac{1}{5001} \approx 0.0002 .pperm​=5000+10+1​=50011​≈0.0002.

Figure 5 shows the observed slope sitting off the right edge of the whole permutation histogram.

Interpretation. Both methods say the same thing: the Toluca slope is real beyond any reasonable doubt. But notice a limit of the permutation test. With 5000 reshuffles, the smallest p-value it can report is 1/5001≈0.00021/5001 \approx 0.00021/5001≈0.0002. It cannot produce the ttt test’s 4.45×10−104.45 \times 10^{-10}4.45×10−10, not because the two disagree, but because a Monte Carlo permutation test has a floor set by how many reshuffles you run. When the evidence is this strong, the permutation test says “smaller than I can measure,” and the ttt test, if you trust its assumptions, puts a number on just how small.

The floor 1/(B+1)1/(B+1)1/(B+1) raises a fair question: how many reshuffles is enough? Because ppermp_{\text{perm}}pperm​ is a Monte Carlo estimate, it wobbles from run to run when BBB is small and settles as BBB grows. Figure 6 tracks the running punting p-value against the number of reshuffles used, for three different random seeds. Past a few thousand reshuffles the three lines have nearly stopped moving, which is why this chapter uses B=5000B = 5000B=5000. If you needed to resolve a p-value near a sharp cutoff, you would run more.

Try it 5.3

You run a permutation test with B=999B = 999B=999 reshuffles and none of them beats your observed statistic. What p-value do you report, and what would you have to do to report something smaller?

Solution

With M=0M = 0M=0 out of B=999B = 999B=999, the permutation p-value is (0+1)/(999+1)=1/1000=0.001(0 + 1)/(999 + 1) = 1/1000 = 0.001(0+1)/(999+1)=1/1000=0.001. You cannot honestly report anything smaller from this run, because 1/(B+1)1/(B+1)1/(B+1) is the floor. To resolve a smaller p-value you must run more reshuffles (raise BBB), which lowers the floor, or switch to a method like the ttt test that puts an analytic number on the far tail, accepting its assumptions in exchange.

5.4 The bootstrap for regression¶

Intuition¶

A permutation test answers “is there an effect?” It does not tell you how big the effect is or how precise your estimate of it is. For that we want the sampling distribution of b1b_1b1​: the spread of slopes you would see if you could repeat the whole study many times. We cannot repeat the study. The bootstrap fakes the repetition using the one sample we have, by resampling it with replacement. The idea, due to Bradley Efron, is that the sample is our best picture of the population, so drawing new samples from the sample imitates drawing new samples from the population.

Definition 5.4: Bootstrap

The bootstrap estimates the sampling variability of a statistic by resampling the observed data with replacement many times, recomputing the statistic on each resample, and treating the spread of those values as an estimate of how much the statistic would vary across repeated studies. It rests on the principle that the sample is the best available stand-in for the population.

Key idea

A permutation test and a bootstrap answer different questions. Permutation shuffling breaks the link between predictor and response to ask whether any effect exists at all, and it returns a p-value. Bootstrap resampling imitates repeated studies to ask how precise the effect estimate is, and it returns a standard error or confidence interval. Reach for the first to detect an effect, the second to measure one.

There are two natural ways to resample a regression, and they answer slightly different questions. Figure 7 shows both. Case resampling draws whole rows (Xi,Yi)(X_i, Y_i)(Xi​,Yi​) with replacement, so a given punter may appear twice or not at all, and the set of strength values changes from resample to resample. This treats the punters as a random sample from a population of punters. Residual resampling keeps the strengths and the fitted line fixed, then builds a new response by adding reshuffled-with-replacement residuals back onto the fitted values. This treats the strength values as fixed by design and puts all the randomness in the errors, matching the regression model of Chapter 2 more literally.

Definition 5.5: Case resampling and residual resampling

Case resampling draws whole rows (Xi,Yi)(X_i, Y_i)(Xi​,Yi​) with replacement, so the set of predictor values varies from resample to resample; it fits data in which the subjects were randomly sampled. Residual resampling holds the predictors XiX_iXi​ and the fitted line Y^i=b0+b1Xi\hat Y_i = b_0 + b_1 X_iY^i​=b0​+b1​Xi​ fixed and rebuilds the response as Yi∗=Y^i+es(i)∗Y_i^{\ast} = \hat Y_i + e_{s(i)}^{\ast}Yi∗​=Y^i​+es(i)∗​, where each es(i)∗e_{s(i)}^{\ast}es(i)∗​ is a residual drawn with replacement from e1,…,ene_1, \dots, e_ne1​,…,en​; it fits data in which the predictor values are fixed by design.

Formula¶

Both bootstraps repeat a resample-and-refit loop BBB times and collect the slopes. The case-resampling algorithm is:

for b=1,…,B:draw (Xi∗,Yi∗) by sampling n rows with replacement, then b1∗b=Sxy∗Sxx∗.\text{for } b = 1, \dots, B: \quad \text{draw } (X_i^{\ast}, Y_i^{\ast}) \text{ by sampling } n \text{ rows with replacement, then } b_1^{\ast b} = \frac{S_{xy}^{\ast}}{S_{xx}^{\ast}} .for b=1,…,B:draw (Xi∗​,Yi∗​) by sampling n rows with replacement, then b1∗b​=Sxx∗​Sxy∗​​.

In words: rebuild a dataset by drawing nnn rows at random with repeats allowed, and refit. The residual-resampling algorithm fixes XiX_iXi​ and the observed fit Y^i=b0+b1Xi\hat Y_i = b_0 + b_1 X_iY^i​=b0​+b1​Xi​ with residuals ei=Yi−Y^ie_i = Y_i - \hat Y_iei​=Yi​−Y^i​:

for b=1,…,B:Yi∗=Y^i+es(i)∗,b1∗b=∑(Xi−Xˉ)(Yi∗−Yˉ∗)Sxx,\text{for } b = 1, \dots, B: \quad Y_i^{\ast} = \hat Y_i + e_{s(i)}^{\ast}, \quad b_1^{\ast b} = \frac{\sum (X_i - \bar X)(Y_i^{\ast} - \bar Y^{\ast})}{S_{xx}} ,for b=1,…,B:Yi∗​=Y^i​+es(i)∗​,b1∗b​=Sxx​∑(Xi​−Xˉ)(Yi∗​−Yˉ∗)​,

where es(i)∗e_{s(i)}^{\ast}es(i)∗​ is a residual drawn at random with replacement from e1,…,ene_1, \dots, e_ne1​,…,en​. In words: keep the line and the strengths, then jiggle each fitted point up or down by a residual borrowed from somewhere in the data. The bootstrap standard error of the slope is the standard deviation of the collected slopes,

sboot{b1}=1B−1∑b=1B(b1∗b−bˉ1∗)2,s_{\text{boot}}\{b_1\} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{B-1}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\left(b_1^{\ast b} - \bar b_1^{\ast}\right)^2} ,sboot​{b1​}=B−11​b=1∑B​(b1∗b​−bˉ1∗​)2​,

the ordinary spread of the BBB resampled slopes. It estimates how much b1b_1b1​ would vary across repeated studies.

Definition 5.6: Bootstrap standard error

The bootstrap standard error of a statistic is the standard deviation of its values across the BBB bootstrap resamples, sboot{b1}=sd⁡(b1∗1,…,b1∗B)s_{\text{boot}}\{b_1\} = \operatorname{sd}(b_1^{\ast 1}, \dots, b_1^{\ast B})sboot​{b1​}=sd(b1∗1​,…,b1∗B​). It estimates the true sampling standard deviation of the statistic without any distributional formula.

R¶

Case resampling is a loop that resamples row indices. We reuse the slope function from before.

set.seed(4210)
n <- nrow(punting)
boot_case <- numeric(B)
for (i in 1:B) {
  idx <- sample(n, n, replace = TRUE)  # resample whole rows
  boot_case[i] <- slope(x[idx], y[idx])
}
c(boot_mean = mean(boot_case), boot_SE = sd(boot_case))
boot_mean   boot_SE 
0.9045003 0.2485204

Residual resampling keeps x fixed and rebuilds y from the fitted values plus resampled residuals.

b0_obs <- mean(y) - b1_obs * mean(x)
fitted_vals <- b0_obs + b1_obs * x
resid_obs <- y - fitted_vals

set.seed(4210)
boot_resid <- numeric(B)
for (i in 1:B) {
  y_star <- fitted_vals + sample(resid_obs, n, replace = TRUE)
  boot_resid[i] <- slope(x, y_star)
}
c(boot_mean = mean(boot_resid), boot_SE = sd(boot_resid))
boot_mean   boot_SE 
0.9020770 0.1976297

Python¶

rng = np.random.default_rng(4210)
n = len(punting)
boot_case = np.empty(B)
for i in range(B):
    idx = rng.integers(0, n, n)           # resample whole rows
    boot_case[i] = slope(x[idx], y[idx])
print("boot_mean =", boot_case.mean(), " boot_SE =", boot_case.std(ddof=1))
boot_mean = 0.9099216280489136  boot_SE = 0.24681912512413196
b0_obs = y.mean() - b1_obs * x.mean()
fitted_vals = b0_obs + b1_obs * x
resid_obs = y - fitted_vals

rng = np.random.default_rng(4210)
boot_resid = np.empty(B)
for i in range(B):
    y_star = fitted_vals + rng.choice(resid_obs, n, replace=True)
    boot_resid[i] = slope(x, y_star)
print("boot_mean =", boot_resid.mean(), " boot_SE =", boot_resid.std(ddof=1))
boot_mean = 0.9009502801536351  boot_SE = 0.1951940681246211
Example 5.3: Two bootstrap standard errors for the punting slope

Question. How precise is the punting slope 0.902, and does the answer depend on which bootstrap we use?

Intuition. Resample 5000 times each way, collect the slopes, and take their standard deviation.

Formula. sboot{b1}=sd⁡(b1∗1,…,b1∗B)s_{\text{boot}}\{b_1\} = \operatorname{sd}(b_1^{\ast 1}, \dots, b_1^{\ast B})sboot​{b1​}=sd(b1∗1​,…,b1∗B​).

Computation. Case resampling gives a bootstrap standard error of about 0.249 (R) or 0.247 (Python); residual resampling gives about 0.198 (R) or 0.195 (Python). The classical formula standard error from the fit is 0.210. Figure 8 draws both bootstrap distributions with the normal-theory curve overlaid.

Interpretation. The three standard errors, 0.249, 0.210, and 0.198, tell a consistent story about a slope of 0.902: the effect is several standard errors from zero no matter how you measure the spread. The case bootstrap is the widest because it adds one more source of variability, the changing set of strength values, that the other two hold fixed. When the punters are a sample from some larger population and you care about that population, case resampling is the honest choice, and its slightly larger standard error is a feature, not a flaw. When you regard the strength values as fixed by the study design, residual resampling matches the classical model and gives a standard error close to the formula’s.

Durable skill: Get a standard error by simulating the study

A standard error is just the spread of an estimate across repeated studies you did not get to run. The bootstrap runs them for you by resampling the one dataset you have. Once you can write a resample-and-refit loop, you can attach a standard error to almost any statistic, even ones with no textbook formula: a median, a ratio, a difference of correlations, the output of a whole model-fitting pipeline. The skill is recognizing that “how much would this bounce if I did it again?” is a question you can answer by simulation whenever you cannot answer it by algebra.

Try it 5.4

In the residual bootstrap, we add resampled residuals back onto the fitted values from the original fit, and we never refit the intercept and slope before resampling. Explain why the residual bootstrap holds the strength values x fixed across all BBB resamples, while the case bootstrap does not.

Solution

The residual bootstrap builds each new response as Yi∗=Y^i+es(i)∗Y_i^{\ast} = \hat Y_i + e^{\ast}_{s(i)}Yi∗​=Y^i​+es(i)∗​ using the same XiX_iXi​ every time, so the predictor column never changes; only the errors are redrawn. That mirrors the Chapter 2 model, where the XiX_iXi​ are fixed constants and all randomness is in the errors. The case bootstrap instead draws whole rows (Xi,Yi)(X_i, Y_i)(Xi​,Yi​) with replacement, so the set of strength values that appears is different in every resample. That mirrors a study where the punters themselves were sampled at random. The two match different data-collection stories, which is why their standard errors differ.

5.5 Bootstrap confidence intervals¶

Intuition¶

A standard error is a single number for the spread. A confidence interval is a range that should contain the true slope β1\beta_1β1​ with, say, 95%95\%95% confidence. The bootstrap distribution of the slope already contains the information; we just have to read an interval off it. Two simple recipes do this without assuming the distribution is normal (both are collected in Definition 5.7). The percentile interval takes the middle 95%95\%95% of the bootstrap slopes directly. The basic interval (also called the reflection interval) corrects for a subtle bias by flipping the percentiles around the observed slope. When the bootstrap distribution is symmetric they nearly coincide; when it is skewed they part ways, and the basic interval is usually the better behaved of the two.

Formula¶

Let qα/2q_{\alpha/2}qα/2​ and q1−α/2q_{1-\alpha/2}q1−α/2​ be the lower and upper percentiles of the BBB bootstrap slopes, for a 100(1−α)%100(1-\alpha)\%100(1−α)% interval (so α=0.05\alpha = 0.05α=0.05 for 95%95\%95%). The two intervals are

percentile:(qα/2,  q1−α/2),basic:(2b1−q1−α/2,  2b1−qα/2).\text{percentile:} \quad \big(q_{\alpha/2}, \; q_{1-\alpha/2}\big), \qquad\qquad \text{basic:} \quad \big(2 b_1 - q_{1-\alpha/2}, \; 2 b_1 - q_{\alpha/2}\big) .percentile:(qα/2​,q1−α/2​),basic:(2b1​−q1−α/2​,2b1​−qα/2​).
Definition 5.7: Percentile and basic bootstrap intervals

Let qα/2q_{\alpha/2}qα/2​ and q1−α/2q_{1-\alpha/2}q1−α/2​ be the lower and upper percentiles of the BBB bootstrap slopes. The 100(1−α)%100(1-\alpha)\%100(1−α)% percentile interval is (qα/2, q1−α/2)(q_{\alpha/2},\, q_{1-\alpha/2})(qα/2​,q1−α/2​), read straight off the bootstrap distribution. The basic (reflection) interval is (2b1−q1−α/2, 2b1−qα/2)(2 b_1 - q_{1-\alpha/2},\, 2 b_1 - q_{\alpha/2})(2b1​−q1−α/2​,2b1​−qα/2​), the percentiles reflected through twice the observed slope. The two coincide when the bootstrap distribution is symmetric about b1b_1b1​.

In words: the percentile interval is just the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles of the resampled slopes; the basic interval reflects those two percentiles through twice the observed slope. Notice the basic interval swaps the roles of the two percentiles, because reflecting turns the upper tail into the lower endpoint.

That swap is easier to see than to say. Figure 9 draws it. Think of the observed slope b1b_1b1​ as a mirror standing in the middle. The percentile interval sits above; to get the basic interval you reflect each endpoint through the mirror, so the point that was far to the right lands far to the left and vice versa. The arithmetic 2b1−q2b_1 - q2b1​−q is exactly “reflect qqq across b1b_1b1​”: it measures how far qqq sits from b1b_1b1​ and steps the same distance to the other side.

Derivation (the basic bootstrap interval)¶

Theorem 5.8: Basic bootstrap interval

Suppose the bootstrap principle holds, so that b1∗−b1b_1^{\ast} - b_1b1∗​−b1​ has approximately the same distribution as b1−β1b_1 - \beta_1b1​−β1​. If qα/2q_{\alpha/2}qα/2​ and q1−α/2q_{1-\alpha/2}q1−α/2​ are the percentiles of the bootstrap slopes b1∗b_1^{\ast}b1∗​, then

P(2b1−q1−α/2≤β1≤2b1−qα/2)≈1−α,P\big(2 b_1 - q_{1-\alpha/2} \le \beta_1 \le 2 b_1 - q_{\alpha/2}\big) \approx 1 - \alpha,P(2b1​−q1−α/2​≤β1​≤2b1​−qα/2​)≈1−α,

so the basic interval (2b1−q1−α/2, 2b1−qα/2)(2 b_1 - q_{1-\alpha/2},\, 2 b_1 - q_{\alpha/2})(2b1​−q1−α/2​,2b1​−qα/2​) has approximate coverage 1−α1 - \alpha1−α. It equals the percentile interval exactly when the bootstrap distribution is symmetric about b1b_1b1​.

Proof. The bootstrap principle says the variation of the resampled slope around the observed slope imitates the variation of the observed slope around the true slope. Write this as: the distribution of b1∗−b1b_1^{\ast} - b_1b1∗​−b1​ approximates the distribution of b1−β1b_1 - \beta_1b1​−β1​. Let qα/2q_{\alpha/2}qα/2​ and q1−α/2q_{1-\alpha/2}q1−α/2​ be the percentiles of b1∗b_1^{\ast}b1∗​, so by definition

P(qα/2≤b1∗≤q1−α/2)=1−α.P\big(q_{\alpha/2} \le b_1^{\ast} \le q_{1-\alpha/2}\big) = 1 - \alpha .P(qα/2​≤b1∗​≤q1−α/2​)=1−α.

Subtract b1b_1b1​ throughout to center on the observed slope:

P(qα/2−b1≤b1∗−b1≤q1−α/2−b1)=1−α.P\big(q_{\alpha/2} - b_1 \le b_1^{\ast} - b_1 \le q_{1-\alpha/2} - b_1\big) = 1 - \alpha .P(qα/2​−b1​≤b1∗​−b1​≤q1−α/2​−b1​)=1−α.

Now use the bootstrap principle to replace b1∗−b1b_1^{\ast} - b_1b1∗​−b1​ with b1−β1b_1 - \beta_1b1​−β1​, which has approximately the same distribution:

P(qα/2−b1≤b1−β1≤q1−α/2−b1)≈1−α.P\big(q_{\alpha/2} - b_1 \le b_1 - \beta_1 \le q_{1-\alpha/2} - b_1\big) \approx 1 - \alpha .P(qα/2​−b1​≤b1​−β1​≤q1−α/2​−b1​)≈1−α.

Solve the inequalities for β1\beta_1β1​. Subtracting b1b_1b1​ and negating (which flips the inequality directions and swaps the endpoints) gives

P(2b1−q1−α/2≤β1≤2b1−qα/2)≈1−α.P\big(2 b_1 - q_{1-\alpha/2} \le \beta_1 \le 2 b_1 - q_{\alpha/2}\big) \approx 1 - \alpha .P(2b1​−q1−α/2​≤β1​≤2b1​−qα/2​)≈1−α.

That bracket is the basic interval. The percentile interval (qα/2,q1−α/2)(q_{\alpha/2}, q_{1-\alpha/2})(qα/2​,q1−α/2​) is what you get if you skip the pivoting step and read the bootstrap percentiles as if they were percentiles of β1\beta_1β1​ directly. The two agree exactly when the bootstrap distribution is symmetric about b1b_1b1​, because then reflecting the percentiles through 2b12 b_12b1​ lands them back on themselves. ■\blacksquare■

R¶

perc_ci  <- quantile(boot_case, c(0.025, 0.975))
basic_ci <- c(2 * b1_obs - perc_ci[[2]], 2 * b1_obs - perc_ci[[1]])
t_ci     <- confint(fit)["RStr", ]
round(c(perc_lo = perc_ci[[1]], perc_hi = perc_ci[[2]]), 4)
round(c(basic_lo = basic_ci[1], basic_hi = basic_ci[2]), 4)
round(c(t_lo = t_ci[[1]], t_hi = t_ci[[2]]), 4)
perc_lo perc_hi 
 0.4158  1.3883 
basic_lo basic_hi 
  0.4158   1.3883 
  t_lo   t_hi 
0.4398 1.3643

Python¶

perc_ci = np.quantile(boot_case, [0.025, 0.975])
basic_ci = np.array([2 * b1_obs - perc_ci[1], 2 * b1_obs - perc_ci[0]])
t_ci = fit.conf_int().loc["RStr"].to_numpy()
print("percentile", np.round(perc_ci, 4))
print("basic     ", np.round(basic_ci, 4))
print("classical ", np.round(t_ci, 4))
percentile [0.413  1.3824]
basic      [0.4216 1.3911]
classical  [0.4398 1.3643]
Example 5.4: A 95% interval for the punting slope, three ways

Question. What is a 95%95\%95% confidence interval for the punting slope, and do the bootstrap and classical intervals agree?

Intuition. Read the percentile and basic intervals off the case-bootstrap slopes and put them next to the classical ttt interval.

Formula. Percentile (q0.025,q0.975)(q_{0.025}, q_{0.975})(q0.025​,q0.975​); basic (2b1−q0.975,2b1−q0.025)(2b_1 - q_{0.975}, 2b_1 - q_{0.025})(2b1​−q0.975​,2b1​−q0.025​); classical b1±t0.975,n−2∗ s{b1}b_1 \pm t^{\ast}_{0.975, n-2}\, s\{b_1\}b1​±t0.975,n−2∗​s{b1​}.

Computation. From the case bootstrap, R gives percentile (0.416,1.388)(0.416, 1.388)(0.416,1.388) and basic (0.416,1.388)(0.416, 1.388)(0.416,1.388); Python gives percentile (0.413,1.382)(0.413, 1.382)(0.413,1.382) and basic (0.422,1.391)(0.422, 1.391)(0.422,1.391). The classical ttt interval is (0.440,1.364)(0.440, 1.364)(0.440,1.364). Figure 10 draws all three on the bootstrap histogram.

Interpretation. All three intervals say the slope is somewhere around 0.4 to 1.4 feet per pound, comfortably above zero, so the effect is real and its size is estimated to within a factor of about three. The percentile and basic intervals nearly coincide here because the case-bootstrap distribution is close to symmetric about 0.902 (recall Figure 8). The bootstrap intervals are slightly wider than the classical (0.440,1.364)(0.440, 1.364)(0.440,1.364) because the classical interval trusts a normal model that thirteen points cannot confirm, while the case bootstrap pays for that honesty with a little extra width.

Try it 5.5

The observed slope is b1=0.902b_1 = 0.902b1​=0.902 and the Python percentile interval is (0.413,1.382)(0.413, 1.382)(0.413,1.382). Use the reflection formula to compute the basic interval by hand, and check it against the printed (0.4216,1.3911)(0.4216, 1.3911)(0.4216,1.3911).

Solution

The basic interval is (2b1−q0.975,  2b1−q0.025)(2 b_1 - q_{0.975}, \; 2 b_1 - q_{0.025})(2b1​−q0.975​,2b1​−q0.025​) with 2b1=1.80412 b_1 = 1.80412b1​=1.8041. The lower endpoint is 1.8041−1.382=0.42211.8041 - 1.382 = 0.42211.8041−1.382=0.4221 and the upper endpoint is 1.8041−0.413=1.39111.8041 - 0.413 = 1.39111.8041−0.413=1.3911, giving (0.422,1.391)(0.422, 1.391)(0.422,1.391), matching the printed values to rounding. Notice the upper percentile 1.382 became the lower endpoint: reflection swaps the tails.

5.6 When computational and classical inference agree¶

You have now met every tool in the chapter. Before comparing their answers, it helps to see them on one page. Figure 11 is a map: it starts from your fitted slope and asks what you want to know, then walks you to the right method. The first fork is the big one. If you want to know whether an effect exists at all, you run a permutation test and report a p-value. If you want to know how precise your estimate is, you run a bootstrap and report a standard error or confidence interval. The bootstrap then forks again by how the data were collected: case resampling for randomly sampled subjects, residual resampling for a design with fixed predictor values.

You have now seen three verdicts on the punting slope (permutation, bootstrap, classical) that all pointed the same way, and a Toluca slope where they agreed up to the permutation test’s resolution floor. That is the common case: when the classical assumptions roughly hold, the computational and classical answers match, and the agreement is itself evidence that the classical p-value can be trusted. The methods earn their keep when the assumptions are shaky, because then the resampling answer does not depend on those assumptions.

Key idea

When computational and classical inference agree, the agreement is itself evidence that the classical assumptions roughly hold and its p-value can be trusted. When they disagree by a lot, that gap is a diagnostic: it warns that an assumption is failing, and the assumption-free resampling answer is the safer one to report while you investigate.

To see the agreement directly, compare the bootstrap and classical standard errors on the Toluca slope, where n=25n = 25n=25 and the data are clean.

set.seed(4210)
boot_toluca <- numeric(B)
nt <- length(xt)
for (i in 1:B) {
  idx <- sample(nt, nt, replace = TRUE)
  boot_toluca[i] <- slope(xt[idx], yt[idx])
}
classical_se <- summary(lm(hours ~ lotsize, data = toluca))$coefficients["lotsize", "Std. Error"]
c(bootstrap_SE = sd(boot_toluca), classical_SE = classical_se)
bootstrap_SE classical_SE 
   0.3765516    0.3469722 
rng = np.random.default_rng(4210)
nt = len(xt)
boot_toluca = np.empty(B)
for i in range(B):
    idx = rng.integers(0, nt, nt)
    boot_toluca[i] = slope(xt[idx], yt[idx])
classical_se = smf.ols("hours ~ lotsize", data=toluca).fit().bse["lotsize"]
print("bootstrap_SE =", boot_toluca.std(ddof=1), " classical_SE =", classical_se)
bootstrap_SE = 0.36568568108182364  classical_SE = 0.3469721568487608
Example 5.5: Bootstrap and classical standard errors on Toluca

Question. On the clean n=25n = 25n=25 Toluca data, does the case bootstrap reproduce the classical standard error of the slope?

Intuition. Case-resample the Toluca rows 5000 times, take the spread of slopes, and compare to the formula standard error 0.347 from 2.5 Sampling behavior and the Gauss-Markov theorem.

Formula. Compare sboot{b1}s_{\text{boot}}\{b_1\}sboot​{b1​} with s{b1}=MSE/Sxxs\{b_1\} = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}/S_{xx}}s{b1​}=MSE/Sxx​​, where MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE is the mean squared error (the residual variance estimate) from the fit.

Computation. The case bootstrap gives a standard error of about 0.377 (R) or 0.366 (Python), against the classical 0.347.

Interpretation. The two standard errors are close, within about ten percent, which confirms that with a healthy sample the bootstrap and the classical formula measure the same variability. The bootstrap is a touch larger because case resampling adds the variation from the changing lot sizes and because n=25n = 25n=25 is still modest. The lesson is that the bootstrap is not a rival that overturns classical inference when the assumptions hold; it agrees with it, and that agreement is what lets you trust the classical result. The bootstrap matters most when you cannot check the assumptions, exactly the punting situation.

A wrong reading to avoid: a student sees the bootstrap standard error 0.377 next to the classical 0.347 and concludes “the bootstrap is wrong, because it does not match the formula.” That gets it backward. Neither number is the truth; both estimate the same unknown standard deviation of b1b_1b1​, and they differ only by sampling noise and by the small extra variability case resampling includes on purpose. The right reading is that they agree well enough to reinforce each other. You would only worry if they disagreed by a lot, which would be a signal that the classical assumptions are failing and the bootstrap is telling you something the formula cannot.

Try it 5.6

Suppose on some dataset the bootstrap standard error of a slope came out three times larger than the classical formula standard error. What would you suspect, and which number would you be more inclined to trust?

Solution

A large gap means the classical formula is missing a source of variability. Likely culprits are strong non-constant error variance, a few high-influence points whose repeated appearance in case resamples swings the slope, or a badly non-normal error distribution in a small sample. In that situation the classical standard error, which assumes constant variance and leans on normality, is probably too small, and the case-bootstrap standard error, which makes neither assumption, is the more trustworthy of the two. The disagreement is a diagnostic: it tells you to investigate the assumptions before reporting the classical interval. Chapters 9 and 10 build the tools to find and fix the underlying problem.

5.7 Chapter summary¶

You can now do inference for a regression slope without leaning on the normal error assumption. When a small sample puts the ttt and FFF tests on thin ice and thirteen residuals cannot confirm normality, you replace the untestable assumption with a computation. A permutation test shuffles the response against the fixed predictor to build the no-association null of the slope and reports pperm=(M+1)/(B+1)p_{\text{perm}} = (M+1)/(B+1)pperm​=(M+1)/(B+1); on the punting data it gave ≈0.001\approx 0.001≈0.001, matching the classical 0.00127. A bootstrap resamples the data with replacement to measure precision: case resampling and residual resampling gave slope standard errors of about 0.249 and 0.198, and percentile and basic intervals near (0.42,1.39)(0.42, 1.39)(0.42,1.39), all consistent with the classical (0.440,1.364)(0.440, 1.364)(0.440,1.364). The recurring lesson is that when computational and classical answers agree the agreement builds trust, and when they diverge sharply that divergence is a warning about assumptions.

Key results at a glance.

ResultStatement or formulaValid when
Permutation test (Definition 5.1)Shuffle YYY against fixed XXX, refit, collect slopesNo-association null; any error distribution
Permutation p-value (Definition 5.2)pperm=(M+1)/(B+1)p_{\text{perm}} = (M+1)/(B+1)pperm​=(M+1)/(B+1)MMM of BBB reshuffles at least as extreme
Validity of the permutation p-value (Theorem 5.3)P(pperm≤α)≤αP(p_{\text{perm}} \le \alpha) \le \alphaP(pperm​≤α)≤α; floor 1/(B+1)1/(B+1)1/(B+1)Under the no-association null
Bootstrap (Definition 5.4)Resample with replacement, recompute, take the spreadSample stands in for the population
Case vs residual resampling (Definition 5.5)Rows with replacement, or Y^i+es(i)∗\hat Y_i + e^{\ast}_{s(i)}Y^i​+es(i)∗​Random subjects, or fixed-design predictor
Bootstrap standard error (Definition 5.6)sboot{b1}=sd⁡(b1∗1,…,b1∗B)s_{\text{boot}}\{b_1\} = \operatorname{sd}(b_1^{\ast 1}, \dots, b_1^{\ast B})sboot​{b1​}=sd(b1∗1​,…,b1∗B​)BBB resamples of the fitted model
Percentile / basic intervals (Definition 5.7)(qα/2,q1−α/2)(q_{\alpha/2}, q_{1-\alpha/2})(qα/2​,q1−α/2​) / (2b1−q1−α/2,2b1−qα/2)(2b_1 - q_{1-\alpha/2}, 2b_1 - q_{\alpha/2})(2b1​−q1−α/2​,2b1​−qα/2​)Read off the bootstrap distribution
Basic interval by pivoting (Theorem 5.8)Reflect percentiles through 2b12b_12b1​; equals percentile if symmetricBootstrap principle holds

Key terms. Permutation test, permutation distribution, permutation p-value, plus-one correction, bootstrap, case resampling, residual resampling, bootstrap standard error, percentile interval, basic bootstrap interval, exchangeability.

You should now be able to.

  • Explain why normal-theory ttt and FFF inference for a slope can fail, and why a small sample makes the risk worse and normality uncheckable.

  • State the no-association null and describe the exact logic of a permutation test for the slope.

  • Implement the permutation test from scratch in R and Python and compute a permutation p-value.

  • Justify the plus-one correction and the resolution floor 1/(B+1)1/(B+1)1/(B+1), and compare against the classical ttt-test p-value.

  • Distinguish case resampling from residual resampling and code each one.

  • Construct and interpret percentile and basic bootstrap confidence intervals for the slope.

  • Decide when computational and classical inference agree, and what to report when they disagree.

Where this fits. On the workflow spine of The modeling workflow, this chapter lives in USE: it is inference, turning a fitted slope into a defensible statement about the world. It is also a quiet CHECK, because comparing computational and classical answers audits the assumptions behind the classical ones. Chapter 3 gave the classical ttt and FFF inference that assumes normal errors; this chapter gave the resampling inference that does not, and showed the two agree when the assumptions hold. The bootstrap returns later as a working tool: Chapter 9 reaches for it when diagnostics say normality has failed, and Chapter 12 uses the same resample-and-refit mindset to validate models on data they were not fit to (12.4 Validation: train, test, and cross-validate). The habit you built here, generate the distribution instead of assuming it, is one you will use for the rest of the book.

5.8 Frequently asked questions¶

Q1. Is a permutation test the same as a bootstrap? No, though both resample. A permutation test shuffles without replacement to break the link between XXX and YYY, building the distribution of a statistic under the null hypothesis of no association; it answers “is there an effect?” The bootstrap resamples with replacement to imitate repeated sampling from the population, building the distribution of the estimate itself; it answers “how precise is the effect?” Use a permutation test for a p-value, a bootstrap for a standard error or confidence interval.

Q2. Why shuffle without replacement for the permutation test but with replacement for the bootstrap? The permutation test needs every reshuffle to be a genuine rearrangement of the same values, so that it represents one of the equally likely pairings under the null; sampling without replacement (a shuffle) does exactly that. The bootstrap needs each resample to look like a fresh draw from the population, and real repeated samples can contain repeats and omissions, so it samples with replacement.

Q3. How many reshuffles or resamples do I need? Enough that the answer stops moving. For a p-value you are comparing to 0.05, a few thousand reshuffles is usually plenty; near a sharp cutoff, use more, because the Monte Carlo floor is 1/(B+1)1/(B+1)1/(B+1) and the noise shrinks like 1/B1/\sqrt{B}1/B​. For a bootstrap standard error, 1000 to 2000 resamples often suffice; for the tail percentiles of a confidence interval, use 5000 or more, since the extreme quantiles are noisier than the center.

Q4. My permutation p-value is 0. Do I report p=0p = 0p=0? Never report exactly zero. If no reshuffle out of BBB beat your statistic, the plus-one correction gives pperm=1/(B+1)p_{\text{perm}} = 1/(B+1)pperm​=1/(B+1), and you report “less than” that: for B=5000B = 5000B=5000, report p<0.0002p < 0.0002p<0.0002. Reporting p=0p = 0p=0 would claim infinite certainty from finite data.

Q5. Which bootstrap should I use, case or residual? Match the resampling to how the data arose. If the predictor values were fixed by design (a planned experiment with set doses, or the Toluca lot sizes chosen by schedulers), residual resampling fits the model literally. If the rows are a random sample from a population and the predictor is just another measured variable (the punters), case resampling is more honest. It is also the safer default when you are unsure, because it captures variability in the predictor and does not assume constant error variance.

Q6. The bootstrap and the ttt test gave slightly different intervals. Which is right? Neither is “right” in an absolute sense; both estimate the same true uncertainty. When they are close, as in the punting and Toluca examples, trust either and note the agreement. When they differ a lot, the disagreement is information: it usually means a classical assumption (normality, constant variance, no influential points) is failing, and the assumption-free bootstrap is the safer report while you investigate.

Q7. Do these methods work for multiple regression? Yes. The bootstrap generalizes directly: case-resample whole rows, or residual-resample around the fitted surface, and collect whichever coefficient you care about. Permutation is subtler with several predictors, because shuffling one predictor while holding others fixed requires care about what null you are testing, but the core idea carries over. This chapter keeps to simple regression so the logic stays visible; the resampling code changes little when you add predictors.

5.9 Practice problems¶

Note

Unless a problem says otherwise, use punting.csv with the model Distance ~ RStr (slope b1=0.902b_1 = 0.902b1​=0.902, classical s{b1}=0.210s\{b_1\} = 0.210s{b1​}=0.210, n=13n = 13n=13), or toluca.csv with hours ~ lotsize (slope b1=3.5702b_1 = 3.5702b1​=3.5702, n=25n = 25n=25). Set seeds (set.seed(4210) in R, np.random.default_rng(4210) in Python) so your resampling answers are reproducible. Problems are marked (A) concepts, (B) theory, or (C) data analysis. Odd-numbered answers appear in Appendix H; full solutions are in the instructor materials.

  1. (A) In one sentence each, state what question a permutation test answers and what question a bootstrap answers, and say which resamples with replacement.

  2. (A) Explain why thirteen residuals cannot confirm or rule out normal errors, referring to Figure 2.

  3. (A) State the null and alternative hypotheses of the permutation test for a slope, in words and in symbols.

  4. (A) Why does the permutation p-value use the plus-one correction (M+1)/(B+1)(M+1)/(B+1)(M+1)/(B+1) instead of M/BM/BM/B? What goes wrong if a test can report exactly zero?

  5. (A) A permutation test with B=4999B = 4999B=4999 reshuffles finds M=0M = 0M=0. What p-value do you report, and what is the smallest p-value this run could ever give?

  6. (A) Describe in words the difference between case resampling and residual resampling, and give one data-collection story that fits each.

  7. (A) The percentile interval for a slope is (1.1,2.9)(1.1, 2.9)(1.1,2.9) and the observed slope is b1=2.2b_1 = 2.2b1​=2.2. Without a computer, find the basic interval.

  8. (A) A classmate reports a permutation p-value of 0 for a slope. Rewrite their conclusion so it is defensible, assuming they used B=9999B = 9999B=9999 reshuffles.

  9. (A) Explain why shuffling YYY against fixed XXX, shuffling XXX against fixed YYY, and shuffling both give the same permutation test.

  10. (B) Show that when you permute the response, the reshuffled slope b1π=Sxyπ/Sxxb_1^{\pi} = S_{xy}^{\pi}/S_{xx}b1π​=Sxyπ​/Sxx​ is a fixed positive constant times the reshuffled cross-product SxyπS_{xy}^{\pi}Sxyπ​, and conclude that the permutation test on the slope is identical to the permutation test on the sample correlation.

  11. (B) Derive the basic bootstrap interval (2b1−q1−α/2, 2b1−qα/2)(2b_1 - q_{1-\alpha/2}, \, 2b_1 - q_{\alpha/2})(2b1​−q1−α/2​,2b1​−qα/2​) (Theorem 5.8) from the bootstrap principle that b1∗−b1b_1^{\ast} - b_1b1∗​−b1​ has approximately the same distribution as b1−β1b_1 - \beta_1b1​−β1​. Show every step of the algebra that flips the endpoints.

  12. (B) Prove that the percentile and basic intervals coincide exactly when the bootstrap distribution of b1∗b_1^{\ast}b1∗​ is symmetric about b1b_1b1​.

  13. (B) The exact two-sided permutation p-value counts arrangements with ∣b1π∣≥∣b1∣|b_1^{\pi}| \ge |b_1|∣b1π​∣≥∣b1​∣ out of n!n!n!. Explain why the observed arrangement is always counted, and use this to argue that the exact p-value is at least 1/n!1/n!1/n!.

  14. (B) A statistic TTT has permutation distribution placing probability 1/n!1/n!1/n! on each arrangement. Explain why, under the null, the random variable ppermp_{\text{perm}}pperm​ (computed from the full enumeration) satisfies P(pperm≤α)≤αP(p_{\text{perm}} \le \alpha) \le \alphaP(pperm​≤α)≤α for any cutoff α\alphaα that is a multiple of 1/n!1/n!1/n!, so the test controls its error rate (Theorem 5.3).

  15. (B) The Monte Carlo standard error of a bootstrap standard-error estimate shrinks like 1/B1/\sqrt{B}1/B​. Explain qualitatively why doubling the accuracy of a tail confidence-interval endpoint costs four times the resamples.

  16. (B) Explain why the case-bootstrap standard error can exceed the classical formula standard error even when the model is correct, by naming the extra source of variation case resampling includes that the residual bootstrap and the classical formula hold fixed.

  17. (C) Reproduce the punting permutation test in R or Python with set.seed(4210) / default_rng(4210) and B=5000B = 5000B=5000. Report the count of extreme reshuffles and the permutation p-value, and compare to the classical ttt-test p-value 0.00127.

  18. (C) Repeat the punting permutation test with the predictor LStr (left-leg strength) instead of RStr. Report the observed slope, the permutation p-value, and whether your conclusion changes.

  19. (C) Run the Toluca permutation test with B=5000B = 5000B=5000. Report the largest reshuffled slope in size and explain why the permutation p-value cannot go below 1/50011/50011/5001 here.

  20. (C) Compute the case-bootstrap and residual-bootstrap standard errors of the punting slope with B=5000B = 5000B=5000. Report both and explain which is larger and why.

  21. (C) Compute percentile and basic 95%95\%95% intervals for the punting slope from the case bootstrap, and put them next to the classical ttt interval (0.440,1.364)(0.440, 1.364)(0.440,1.364). Comment on the agreement.

  22. (C) Using the punting data and predictor OStr (overall leg strength), build a 90%90\%90% percentile bootstrap interval for the slope (use the 5th and 95th percentiles). Report the interval and interpret it in the units of the problem.

  23. (C) Bootstrap the Toluca slope 5000 times by case resampling and compare the bootstrap standard error to the classical 0.347. State the percentage difference and whether the two agree.

  24. (C) Write a permutation test for the punting slope but use the sample correlation as the statistic instead of the slope. Confirm that the p-value matches the slope-based test (problem 17), illustrating the identity from problem 10.

  25. (C) Increase the punting permutation test to B=50000B = 50000B=50000 reshuffles. Does the p-value estimate change materially from the B=5000B = 5000B=5000 value? Report both and comment on Monte Carlo noise.

  26. (C) Make a normal quantile plot of the punting residuals in R or Python, then generate three normal samples of size 13 and plot their quantile plots. Describe how hard it is to tell the real residuals from the normal samples, connecting to Figure 2.

  27. (C) For the punting slope, compute a bootstrap standard error and form the interval b1±1.96 sboot{b1}b_1 \pm 1.96 \, s_{\text{boot}}\{b_1\}b1​±1.96sboot​{b1​} (a normal-approximation bootstrap interval). Compare it to the percentile interval and explain when the two would disagree.

  28. (B) An analyst uses a paired shuffle: they reorder the rows of the data but keep each (Xi,Yi)(X_i, Y_i)(Xi​,Yi​) pair together, then refit. Show that every such “permutation” gives exactly the observed slope, and explain why this tests nothing.

5.10 Exam practice¶

These five questions match the style of the course exams: each one asks you to explain, evaluate, or interpret in full sentences, not just to compute a number. Write your own answer before opening the model answer. A correct number with no reasoning would earn little credit on the real exam, and a clear explanation with a small arithmetic slip would earn most of it.

EP 5.1 (explain why). The chapter calls the classical ttt test for the punting slope “on thin ice” with n=13n = 13n=13, yet it runs the permutation test on those same thirteen punters without apology. Explain, in full sentences, exactly which assumption the ttt test needs that the permutation test does not, and why having only thirteen punters makes that difference matter here instead of being a technicality.

Model answer

The classical ttt test treats the ratio t∗=b1/s{b1}t^{\ast} = b_1 / s\{b_1\}t∗=b1​/s{b1​} as exactly ttt-distributed on n−2n - 2n−2 degrees of freedom, and that distribution is exact only when the errors are normal. When the errors are not normal, the ttt result holds only as an approximation, and only when the sample is large enough for a central-limit effect to smooth the slope estimate toward normality. With thirteen punters there is no large-sample rescue, and thirteen residuals cannot tell a normal distribution from a non-normal one (Figure 2), so the single premise the ttt test leans on is precisely the one that cannot be checked. That is what “thin ice” means: not that the test is known to be wrong, but that its safety net is unverifiable.

The permutation test needs no distributional assumption at all. Under the no-association null the thirteen distances are attached to the thirteen strengths without regard to them, so the observed slope is literally one of the 13!13!13! equally likely pairings, and reshuffling generates that reference distribution exactly from the data in hand. Normality never enters the argument, so the small sample that undermines the ttt test does not undermine the permutation test. The only price the permutation test pays for small nnn is a coarser reference set (fewer genuinely distinct pairings), not a broken assumption.

A weak answer just says “permutation tests do not assume normality” without explaining that the ttt test’s normality requirement is exactly the uncheckable one at n=13n = 13n=13, and without noting that the permutation reference distribution comes from the equally likely pairings under the null.

EP 5.2 (interpret output and a figure in context). A different physical test, hang time (the seconds the ball stays airborne), is used to predict punt distance with the model Distance ~ Hang. The classical fit and a permutation test with B=5000B = 5000B=5000 (seed 4210) give:

Distance ~ Hang
             Estimate Std. Error  t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept) -22.32579   36.30608 -0.61493  0.55111
Hang         43.50138    9.19420  4.73139  0.00062

permutation test: 2 of 5000 reshuffles as extreme,  p_perm = (2+1)/(5000+1) = 0.0006

Interpret this output and figure in context. State what the slope 43.5 means in the units of the problem, what the two p-values say and whether they agree, and give one sentence of caution about what this analysis does not establish.

Model answer

The slope says that among these thirteen punters, each additional second of hang time goes with about 43.5 more feet of average punt distance. The intercept -22.3 feet is not interpretable on its own, because a hang time of zero is far outside the range of the data and would mean the ball never left the foot. Both p-values are tiny and essentially identical: the classical ttt test gives 0.00062 and the permutation test gives (2+1)/(5000+1)≈0.0006(2 + 1)/(5000 + 1) \approx 0.0006(2+1)/(5000+1)≈0.0006. The figure shows why they agree, because the observed slope 43.5 sits off the far right edge of the reshuffled null, beaten by only two of five thousand shuffles. So a slope this steep almost never arises when hang time and distance are unrelated, and we have strong evidence that the two are associated, a conclusion that does not lean on normal errors because the assumption-free permutation test lands in the same place as the ttt test.

The caution: this is an observational association measured within thirteen punters, not a causal effect. It does not show that a punter could lengthen a kick by deliberately keeping the ball in the air longer, because hang time and distance are both downstream of the same leg strength and technique, and with only thirteen points the estimate 43.5 is itself imprecise.

A weak answer reads the p-value as “significant” but fails to interpret the slope in the units of the problem, does not note that the two methods agree and why that agreement matters, or slides into a causal claim.

EP 5.3 (a student claims something, evaluate it). A student runs the punting permutation test (Distance ~ RStr, B=5000B = 5000B=5000) twice, once with set.seed(4210) and once with set.seed(2026), and gets:

seed 4210:  M = 4 extreme reshuffles,  p_perm = 0.00100
seed 2026:  M = 8 extreme reshuffles,  p_perm = 0.00180

The student concludes: “The permutation test is unreliable, because it gives a different answer every time I run it. I should just report the classical ttt test’s p-value of 0.00127, which is exact and reproducible.” Evaluate the student’s reasoning. Say what is right and what is wrong in each half of the claim.

Model answer

The student is half right and half wrong. It is true that a Monte Carlo permutation p-value wobbles from run to run, because it estimates the true permutation p-value from a finite random sample of BBB reshuffles. The values 0.00100 and 0.00180 are two such estimates, and the gap between them is ordinary Monte Carlo noise, which shrinks like 1/B1/\sqrt{B}1/B​ and would nearly disappear if the student ran far more reshuffles or enumerated all 13!13!13! pairings. But “different every time” is not the same as “unreliable.” Both runs place the p-value near 0.001, both are far below any usual cutoff, and both lead to exactly the same decision, so the conclusion is not in doubt. The reproducibility worry is also already solved by fixing the seed, which the student did.

The second half of the claim is where the reasoning fails. The classical 0.00127 is “exact” only if its normality assumption holds, and the whole reason the chapter uses the punting data is that thirteen residuals cannot confirm normality. So that number is not more trustworthy than the permutation p-value, only more precise-looking. The honest report is the permutation p-value (about 0.001) together with the note that it agrees with the ttt test, or a permutation run with a larger BBB if a sharper figure is wanted.

A weak answer says only “it is random, so that is fine” without naming Monte Carlo error, the 1/B1/\sqrt{B}1/B​ rate, or the fact that both runs give the same decision, and it fails to challenge the false claim that the ttt test’s p-value is exact here.

EP 5.4 (a student claims something, evaluate it). The chapter’s RStr example found the case-bootstrap standard error (0.249) larger than the residual-bootstrap one (0.198), and explained that case resampling “adds one more source of variability.” A student turns this into a rule: “Case resampling always gives a larger standard error than residual resampling, because it lets the predictor values vary too.” To test the rule they refit punt distance on overall leg strength (OStr) with B=5000B = 5000B=5000 and get:

Distance ~ OStr
classical formula standard error   = 0.0991
case-resampling bootstrap SE       = 0.0889
residual-resampling bootstrap SE   = 0.0910

Evaluate the student’s rule in light of this output.

Model answer

The rule is false as stated, and this output is a clean counterexample. Here the case-bootstrap standard error (0.089) is actually smaller than the residual-bootstrap one (0.091), the reverse of the RStr pattern. The chapter’s explanation described a tendency, not a guarantee. Case resampling and residual resampling estimate the same underlying sampling variability of the slope by two different mechanisms, and which one comes out larger on any given dataset depends on the particular residuals and high-leverage points that happen to get resampled, plus ordinary Monte Carlo noise. With only thirteen points these accidents easily swamp the “extra source of variation” argument.

The sound reading is that the three standard errors (0.089, 0.091, and 0.099) all agree to within about ten percent and tell the same story about a slope of 0.43, so the choice between the two bootstraps should be made on the data-collection story (case resampling when the punters are a random sample from a population, residual resampling when the strengths are treated as fixed by design), not on which one happens to yield the bigger number.

A weak answer simply declares the rule right or wrong without using the printed numbers to show that case is smaller than residual here, or without explaining that the “extra variation” effect is only a tendency that small-sample noise can reverse.

EP 5.5 (what would change if). For the punting slope Distance ~ RStr (observed b1=0.902b_1 = 0.902b1​=0.902), answer each part in full sentences.

(a) The chapter uses B=5000B = 5000B=5000 reshuffles and reports pperm≈0.001p_{\text{perm}} \approx 0.001pperm​≈0.001. What would change about the number you could honestly report if you reran the permutation test with only B=500B = 500B=500 reshuffles and again found no reshuffle beating the observed slope?

(b) The chapter case-resamples the punters because they are treated as a sample from a larger population of punters. What would change about which bootstrap is the honest choice if instead these thirteen were the entire group you cared about, say the complete roster of one team, and you only wanted to describe the line within that fixed group?

Model answer

(a) With B=500B = 500B=500 and M=0M = 0M=0, the permutation p-value would be (0+1)/(500+1)=1/501≈0.002(0 + 1)/(500 + 1) = 1/501 \approx 0.002(0+1)/(500+1)=1/501≈0.002, so the smallest number you could honestly report roughly doubles from the B=5000B = 5000B=5000 floor of 1/5001≈0.00021/5001 \approx 0.00021/5001≈0.0002. Fewer reshuffles raise the resolution floor 1/(B+1)1/(B + 1)1/(B+1), so you lose the ability to resolve very small p-values and your estimate is noisier from run to run, even though the qualitative conclusion (strong evidence, p well below 0.05) does not change. To report anything smaller you would have to run more reshuffles.

(b) If the thirteen punters are the whole group and you only want the line within that fixed set, the case for case resampling weakens. Case resampling imitates the randomness of drawing subjects from a population, and there is no such randomness left once you already have everyone. The variability worth modeling is then the scatter of the distances around the line, which is exactly what residual resampling captures by holding the strengths fixed and reattaching resampled residuals, so residual resampling (or the classical fixed-design view) becomes the more honest choice. In the extreme where the fit is treated as a pure description of one fixed finite group, inference about a wider population is arguably not even the right question to ask.

A weak answer to (a) misses that it is the floor 1/(B+1)1/(B + 1)1/(B+1) that changes (about 0.002 versus 0.0002) rather than the decision; a weak answer to (b) just repeats “use case resampling” without recognizing that removing the sampling-of-subjects randomness removes the very reason to prefer it.

Chapter game¶

Play the Chapter 5 game

Play the Chapter 5 game on your phone or laptop: 10 quick rounds, no setup. Open the game. It drills the chapter’s core moves: ordering the permutation loop, computing the (M+1)/(B+1) p-value, fitting the real punting line, matching each resampling method to its job, and reflecting a percentile interval into a basic one, with a one-paragraph reason after every round.

Resumen del capítulo (en español)

Este capítulo presenta la inferencia por remuestreo para la pendiente de una regresión, útil cuando la muestra es pequeña y no se puede confiar en el supuesto de errores normales. El ejemplo motivador son los datos de punting: trece pateadores, con la distancia del punt frente a la fuerza de la pierna derecha. La prueba ttt del Capítulo 3 descansa en supuestos que no se pueden verificar, porque trece residuos no distinguen lo normal de lo no normal.

La prueba de permutación responde si existe una asociación. Se mezcla la respuesta contra el predictor fijo muchas veces, rompiendo cualquier vínculo, y se calcula la pendiente en cada mezcla. El valor p de permutación es p=(M+1)/(B+1)p = (M+1)/(B+1)p=(M+1)/(B+1), donde MMM cuenta las mezclas con pendiente tan extrema como la observada. Para los punters, con B=5000B = 5000B=5000, resultó p≈0.001p \approx 0.001p≈0.001, cercano al 0.00127 de la prueba ttt. Para Toluca, ninguna mezcla alcanzó la pendiente real 3.5702, así que p<1/5001p < 1/5001p<1/5001. La corrección de “más uno” evita reportar un valor p de exactamente cero.

El bootstrap mide la precisión de la estimación. El remuestreo de casos toma filas completas con reemplazo; el remuestreo de residuos mantiene fijos los predictores y la recta ajustada, y reasigna residuos con reemplazo. La desviación estándar de las pendientes remuestreadas es el error estándar bootstrap. De ahí se leen dos intervalos: el de percentiles y el básico, que refleja los percentiles a través de 2b12 b_12b1​. Para los punters, ambos dieron cerca de (0.42,1.39)(0.42, 1.39)(0.42,1.39), algo más anchos que el intervalo ttt clásico (0.440,1.364)(0.440, 1.364)(0.440,1.364).

La lección final: cuando los supuestos clásicos se cumplen, los métodos computacionales y clásicos concuerdan, lo que justifica confiar en la prueba ttt. Cuando difieren mucho, es señal de que algún supuesto falla, y el bootstrap, que no lo necesita, es el reporte más seguro.

Part I: Regression from the ground up
4. Correlation
Part II: The linear model in matrix form
6. Matrix algebra for regression
